sources.notes: Hard version of lexicographically minimizing the reachable permutation under the strange operation.
Official hard solution accelerates the greedy with a Fenwick tree over alive values and a segment tree over inverse positions to jump to the next smaller element.

annotation.summary: Compute the lexicographically smallest reachable permutation under the strange triple operation for large n.
annotation.constraints: n is large, so the easy O(n^2) greedy must be optimized to O(n log n).
annotation.core_idea: Use the same greedy target-fixing logic as F1. Maintain alive values with a Fenwick tree to query the current rank parity of a position's value. If the rank is odd, the current index can be fixed immediately. If it is even, jump to the next smaller value to its right using a segment tree over inverse positions. Each index is visited at most twice, so these jumps plus Fenwick/segment-tree updates yield O(n log n).
annotation.complexity: O(n log n) per test case.
tricks: jump to next smaller value on the right
each index visited at most twice

solution_variants.name: fenwick-segment-tree-greedy
solution_variants.summary: Run the lexicographic greedy while using Fenwick rank queries and segment-tree next-smaller jumps to avoid quadratic scans.

tags.evidence: The same left-to-right lexicographic greedy from the easy version remains the main correctness idea.
A Fenwick tree maintains alive values and answers current rank parity queries.
A segment tree over inverse positions finds the next smaller alive value to the right.
The reachable lexicographic minimum is determined by ranks and relative positions of consecutive values.
